old_claim.note: Aqueducts provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Inspection and maintenance of urban sewers presupposes an existing sewer/drainage system; the Cloaca Maxima entry describes the sewer network and Agrippa's inspection of the system.

source_shape.source_locator: Cloaca Maxima entry, lines describing the sewer, its branches/course, and Pliny's report that Agrippa inspected the whole system in 33 BCE.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Platner and Ashby describe the Cloaca Maxima as a sewer/drainage system and report Agrippa's 33 BCE inspection of that sewer system.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The source supports existing sewers/drainage as the direct object and prerequisite of inspection. It does not make aqueducts necessary; aqueducts are neighboring water-supply infrastructure.

ontology_before: The graph treated aqueducts as an enabler of sewer inspection, conflating water-supply infrastructure with sewer/drainage maintenance.
ontology_after: Urban sewer inspection is anchored to documented inspection of existing Roman sewer/drainage infrastructure and points to sewers_and_drainage as the required upstream system.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Graph invariant 2026-06-10-urban-sewer-inspection-scope keeps aqueducts absent and sewers_and_drainage present as the required dependency.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to aqueduct inspection or aqueduct-maintenance crews.
A source showed the first scoped sewer-inspection practice required aqueduct infrastructure rather than sewers/drainage infrastructure.
A later graph split introduced a combined Roman waterworks inspection office node that separately covered aqueducts, reservoirs, and sewers.
short_reason: Sewer inspection depends on sewers/drainage, not aqueducts.
